skill.name: internal-comms
skill.description: Draft internal company communications using provided formats for 3P updates, newsletters, FAQs, status updates, and leadership updates.
skill.summary: Create concise internal communications from user-provided or authorized company context.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives caused by ordinary prose, Markdown code spans, and Apache License text. I found no prompt injection, executable code, command execution, credential access, or data exfiltration logic. The skill does guide use of authorized internal sources, so users should limit inputs to appropriate company information.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged ordinary guidance text, but the line describes newsletter attributes. No cryptographic algorithm or security implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected line is prose for communication formatting. There is no code path, algorithm selection, or cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged ordinary sentence text about using the first person plural. It does not run identity, host, or environment discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only tells the assistant to use the word "we" in company updates. No command, tool call, or reconnaissance intent exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: A duplicate static alert on the same prose line is not a real system reconnaissance issue. The content is writing style guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is the same harmless prose context as the prior alert. It contains no executable instruction to inspect the system.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched normal communication guidance about clarifying desired tone. No weak cryptography is configured or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line asks the assistant to clarify tone. The weak-crypto signal is a substring match inside ordinary English text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL
low_findings.description: The URL is the standard Apache License homepage in license text. It is not used by code and does not create a network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is static license boilerplate. No executable code references the URL or sends data to it.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL
low_findings.description: The URL is the standard Apache License copy location in the license appendix. It is not operational network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is legal text. It does not define an endpoint, webhook, telemetry target, or fetch behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched wording in Apache License contribution language. The line is legal boilerplate, not cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is the phrase about material designated as not a contribution. It has no security implementation meaning.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched Apache License redistribution language. No cryptographic algorithm is mentioned or used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line begins a NOTICE file clause. It contains no code or cryptographic decision.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched ordinary license wording about describing origin. This is not cryptographic functionality.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is legal prose in the trademarks section. There is no executable or configuration context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched warranty disclaimer text. No encryption, hashing, or signing behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected line is Apache License boilerplate. It cannot influence runtime security behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched the Apache License warranty disclaimer. The line has no cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is legal warranty language. It does not reference a weak algorithm in a technical setting.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched Apache License appendix wording about including a file or class name. This is legal instruction text only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of license application guidance. There is no security-sensitive code or configuration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The backticks are Markdown formatting around a directory name. They are not shell or Ruby command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line instructs the model to load guideline files from examples/. Markdown code spans are not executable commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The backticks format an example file path for 3P updates. There is no instruction to execute the path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is documentation syntax around a relative Markdown file path. No shell interpolation or command runner exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The backticks format an example file path for newsletters. The skill only asks the assistant to read guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected content is a Markdown bullet with a path. It is not a Ruby or shell execution construct.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The backticks format an example file path for FAQ answers. No external command is launched.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is no code interpreter, command API, or user-controlled shell string. It is static documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The backticks format an example file path for general communications. This is safe Markdown path notation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a relative Markdown file path in a code span. No command execution behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched text inside the skill description. The description lists communication types and does not discuss cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is YAML front matter prose. There is no algorithm, library, or security primitive to evaluate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched ordinary clarification guidance. No weak cryptography is configured, recommended, or executed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line tells the assistant to ask for clarification when needed. The alert is caused by normal English text.

low_findings.title: Low Risk: Authorized Internal Source Handling
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to use Slack, email, calendar, documents, and press inputs when available. This is legitimate for internal communications, but generated drafts may include sensitive information if users grant broad access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files explicitly direct use of internal company sources. This is intended behavior, but it creates a real confidentiality concern if source access is too broad.

content.value_statement: Internal updates often need consistent structure and careful tone. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft concise company communications from authorized context.
actual_capabilities: Drafts 3P updates with Progress, Plans, and Problems sections.
Creates company newsletter drafts with concise grouped bullets.
Writes FAQ answers based on common employee questions.
Prepares general internal communications after clarifying audience, purpose, tone, and format.
Suggests source types such as Slack, email, calendar events, documents, and press mentions when access is available.
Uses provided example formats to keep drafts short and structured.
limitations: Does not verify facts unless the user provides sources or authorized tool access.
Does not decide what confidential information may be shared.
Does not send messages, emails, or posts on its own.
Does not replace legal, HR, security, or executive review for sensitive announcements.

use_cases.title: Weekly Team Update
use_cases.description: Turn recent progress, next plans, and blockers into a short 3P update for leadership.

use_cases.title: Company Newsletter Draft
use_cases.description: Convert company-wide announcements and milestones into a structured newsletter for Slack and email.

use_cases.title: Employee FAQ Response
use_cases.description: Summarize common employee questions and draft clear answers with links to official sources.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a 3P Update
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with direct source notes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a 3P update for the Product team for this week. Progress: [notes]. Plans: [notes]. Problems: [notes]. Keep it concise.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Newsletter
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a company-wide newsletter from these announcements: [paste notes]. Group related items and make each bullet one or two sentences.

prompt_templates.title: Answer Employee FAQs
prompt_templates.prompt: Write FAQ answers for these employee questions: [questions]. Use official information from these sources: [links or notes]. Mark uncertainty clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Synthesize Authorized Sources
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request using connected workplace tools.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using my authorized Slack, email, calendar, and document context, draft a leadership update for [audience] covering [date range]. Exclude confidential details.

output_examples.input: A product leader needs a weekly 3P update from rough notes about launches, roadmap work, and staffing blockers.
output_examples.output: A short update with one header and three labeled sections: Progress, Plans, and Problems. Each section uses one to three direct sentences.

output_examples.input: An internal communications manager provides company announcements, executive posts, and launch notes.
output_examples.output: A grouped newsletter draft with sections for company announcements, progress on priorities, leadership updates, and social highlights.

output_examples.input: A people operations partner shares repeated employee questions about a policy change.
output_examples.output: A concise FAQ list with one-sentence questions, short answers, source links, and clear notes where executive input is needed.

best_practices: Provide source notes, audience, deadline, and approval requirements before drafting.
Limit connected-source access to information approved for the communication audience.
Review facts, links, names, and sensitive details before publishing any draft.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to infer confidential strategy without approved sources.
Do not publish drafts without human review for sensitive or executive topics.
Do not mix audiences when one message needs different versions for different groups.

faq.question: What types of communications can this skill draft?
faq.answer: It can draft 3P updates, newsletters, FAQ answers, status reports, leadership updates, project updates, and incident updates.

faq.question: Does it require access to company tools?
faq.answer: No. It can use pasted notes, but authorized Slack, email, calendar, or document access can improve context.

faq.question: Can it publish messages automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides drafting guidance only and does not send messages or emails.

faq.question: How should confidential information be handled?
faq.answer: Users should provide only approved information and remove sensitive details before sharing or publishing drafts.

faq.question: Can it answer employee questions from uncertain information?
faq.answer: It can draft cautious answers, but it should mark uncertainty and request official sources when facts are unclear.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
